original_hypothesis.title: GTC.delegateOnDist() centralizes delegated voting power to GTCDist and can be abused if GTCDist is mis-set, enabling governance capture in environments that use this token for voting
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: delegateOnDist() allows a single privileged address (GTCDist) to set delegation for any delegator without their direct on-chain action. If GTCDist is set incorrectly (or later changed by minter), a malicious GTCDist can redirect votes for many holders, enabling governance capture in systems that rely on GTC voting checkpoints.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Although labeled test-only, this is an operationally relevant pattern: distribution contracts often hold privileged delegation rights. If any governance or role assignment in the wider system depends on GTC vote snapshots, misconfiguration or misuse of GTCDist becomes a practical governance threat.
deployment_assumptions: This GTC.sol (or similar) is used in an environment where GTC votes affect governance/permissions
GTCDist is configured and can call delegateOnDist
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: GTC.sol includes setGTCDist() callable by minter; no production wiring evidence provided.

proof_of_signal.pattern: setGTCDist: only minter; delegateOnDist: require(msg.sender == GTCDist) then _delegate(delegator, delegatee)

preconditions: GTCDist is set to an address controlled by attacker or operationally compromised actor
Token voting checkpoints are used for governance decisions somewhere (not shown in this repo)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: A single contract address can unilaterally re-delegate votes across users, which is a powerful centralized lever.
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Depends on minter setting GTCDist (and minter control).
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time before governance snapshot blocks used by external systems.

operational_questions: Is this token ever used in non-test environments where votes matter?
How is minter controlled, and what are the controls around setGTCDist?
Are there external governance systems reading these checkpoints?
validation_requirements: Trace any integration that uses getPriorVotes/getCurrentVotes for authorization
Review who controls minter and whether GTCDist is immutable in practice
Simulate mass delegation changes and measure governance impact

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Gain control of GTCDist (via misconfiguration) or influence minter to set malicious GTCDist
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Call delegateOnDist for many delegators to redirect votes to attacker delegatee
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Use captured voting power in external governance to pass malicious actions

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Indirect: governance capture in systems that depend on token voting
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Medium: governance integrity loss in affected environment
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium: requires governance rollback or token migration
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Any vote-weighted governance using this checkpoint system

execution_requirements_analysis.governance_tokens_needed: 0 (vote control achieved via delegation redirection rather than acquisition)

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Large number of DelegateChanged events originating from GTCDist
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Unexpected vote shifts without corresponding token transfers
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Delegations converging to a single delegatee

validation_result.impact_summary: Filtered before validation: attack_realism is LOW
validation_result.detailed_analysis: This hypothesis was auto-rejected because its attack_realism grade is LOW. Preconditions require actions outside the attacker's control (off-chain compromise, victim cooperation, or admin collusion). No LLM validation was performed.
